items.correct: Experimentation with form (fragmentation, stream of consciousness), focus on subjective interior experience, scepticism toward traditional narrative and absolute truth.
items.misconception: Modernism just means literature written in modern times.
items.random_wrong: Modernism is literature about modern technology like phones.
items.why: Modernism is a specific early 20th-century movement (Joyce, Woolf, Eliot), not a synonym for contemporary. Students use it as a vague chronological term, missing its formal experimentation.

items.q: What is the dramatic function of a soliloquy?
items.correct: It reveals a character's inner thoughts directly to the audience while the character is alone on stage — a convention granting access to private psychology.
items.misconception: A soliloquy is any long speech a character gives.
items.random_wrong: A soliloquy is when characters argue with each other.
items.why: Students confuse soliloquy (alone, inner thoughts) with monologue (long speech, possibly to others). The key feature is solitude and direct access to thought — losing this loses the convention's force.

items.q: Why are genre conventions important when interpreting a text?
items.correct: They set reader expectations the text can fulfil or subvert; analysing how a text uses or breaks conventions reveals its meaning and significance.
items.misconception: Genre is just a label to help bookshops categorise novels.
items.random_wrong: Genre means whether a book is fiction or non-fiction.
items.why: Genre is interpretively active. A horror novel that subverts horror conventions does so meaningfully — but only if you know the conventions. Students treat genre as labelling, not as a reading lens.
It's Jean Rhys. “Chinua Achebe” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Negritude was founded in which decade?
items.why: Correct answer: 1930s. “1920s” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How many founders of the Negritude movement (Cesaire, Senghor, Damas)?
items.why: Exact answer: 3. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
